heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s

1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
paragraph_or_text_span: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, stan

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.1 Initial assessment for OSAHS
When to suspect OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Take a sleep history and assess people for OSAHS if they have 2 or more of the following features: snoring witnessed apnoeas unrefreshing sleep waking headaches unexplained excessive sleepiness, tiredness or fatigue nocturia (waking from sleep to urinate) choking during sleep sleep fragmentation or insomnia cognitive dysfunction or memory impairment.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.1 Initial assessment for OSAHS
When to suspect OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that there is a higher prevalence of OSAHS in people with any of the following conditions: obesity or overweight obesity or overweight in pregnancy treatment-resistant hypertension type 2 diabetes cardiac arrythmia, particularly atrial fibrillation stroke or transient ischaemic attack chronic heart failure moderate or severe asthma polycystic ovary syndrome Down's syndrome non-arteritic a

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.1 Initial assessment for OSAHS
Assessment scales for suspected OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: When assessing people with suspected OSAHS: Use the Epworth Sleepiness Scale in the preliminary assessment of sleepiness. Consider using the STOP-Bang Questionnaire as well as the Epworth Sleepiness Scale.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.1 Initial assessment for OSAHS
Assessment scales for suspected OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use the Epworth Sleepiness Scale alone to determine if referral is needed, because not all people with OSAHS have excessive sleepiness. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on assessment scales for suspected OSAHS . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in ev

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.2 Prioritising people for rapid assessment by a sleep service
paragraph_or_text_span: See also recommendation 4.1.1 on providing information for people with suspected OSAHS who are being referred to a sleep service .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.2 Prioritising people for rapid assessment by a sleep service
paragraph_or_text_span: When referring people with suspected OSAHS to a sleep service, include the following information in the referral letter to facilitate rapid assessment: results of the person's assessment scores how sleepiness affects the person comorbidities occupational risk oxygen saturation and blood gas values, if available.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.2 Prioritising people for rapid assessment by a sleep service
paragraph_or_text_span: Within the sleep service, prioritise people with suspected OSAHS for rapid assessment if any of the following apply: they have a vocational driving job they have a job for which vigilance is critical for safety they have unstable cardiovascular disease, for example, poorly controlled arrhythmia, nocturnal angina or treatment-resistant hypertension they are pregnant they are undergoing preoperative

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: See also section 4 on providing information for people who have been diagnosed with OSAHS .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer home respiratory polygraphy to people with suspected OSAHS.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: If access to home respiratory polygraphy is limited, consider home oximetry for people with suspected OSAHS. Take into account that oximetry alone may be inaccurate for differentiating between OSAHS and other causes of hypoxaemia in people with heart failure or chronic lung diseases.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider respiratory polygraphy or polysomnography if oximetry results are negative but the person has significant symptoms.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider hospital respiratory polygraphy for people with suspected OSAHS if home respiratory polygraphy and home oximetry are impractical or additional monitoring is needed.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider polysomnography if respiratory polygraphy results are negative but symptoms continue.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.3 Diagnostic tests for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Use the results of the sleep study to diagnose OSAHS and determine the severity of OSAHS (mild, moderate or severe). For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on diagnostic tests for OSAHS . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review D: diagnostic tests for

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.4 Lifestyle advice for all severities of OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Discuss appropriate lifestyle changes with all people with OSAHS. Provide support and information on losing weight, stopping smoking, reducing alcohol intake and improving sleep hygiene, tailored to the person's needs and in line with the NICE guidelines on: tobacco: preventing uptake, promoting quitting and treating dependence overweight and obesity management alcohol-use disorders: prevention (i

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: See also section 4 on providing information for people starting treatment for OSAHS .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Lifestyle advice alone for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people with mild OSAHS who have no symptoms or with symptoms that do not affect usual daytime activities that: treatment is not usually needed and changes to lifestyle and sleep habits (see recommendation 1.4.1 on lifestyle advice) can help to prevent OSAHS from worsening.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Continuous positive airway pressure for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Recommendation 1.5.2 updates recommendation 1.2 in NICE's technology appraisal guidance on continuous positive airway pressure for the treatment of obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Continuous positive airway pressure for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with mild OSAHS who have symptoms that affect their quality of life and usual daytime activities, offer fixed-level continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): at the same time as lifestyle advice if they have any of the priority factors listed in recommendation 1.2.2 or if lifestyle advice alone has been unsuccessful or is considered inappropriate.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Continuous positive airway pressure for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with mild OSAHS having CPAP: Offer telemonitoring with CPAP for up to 12 months. Consider using telemonitoring beyond 12 months.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Continuous positive airway pressure for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider auto‑CPAP as an alternative to fixed-level CPAP in people with mild OSAHS if: high pressure is needed only for certain times during sleep or they are unable to tolerate fixed-level CPAP or telemonitoring cannot be used for technological reasons or auto‑CPAP is available at the same or lower cost than fixed-level CPAP, and this price is guaranteed for an extended period of time.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Continuous positive airway pressure for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider heated humidification for people with mild OSAHS having CPAP who have upper airway side effects, such as nasal and mouth dryness, and CPAP-induced rhinitis.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Continuous positive airway pressure for mild OSAHS
Reducing the risk of transmission of infection when using CPAP
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that CPAP is an aerosol-generating procedure and, if there is a risk of airborne infection, such as COVID‑19, appropriate infection control precautions should be taken. These may include setting up the device at home by video consultation or with precautions in hospital. For more information, see the UK government guidance on COVID-19: infection prevention and control and local guidance.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Mandibular advancement splints for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: If a person with mild OSAHS and symptoms that affect their usual daytime activities is unable to tolerate or declines to try CPAP, consider a customised or semi-customised mandibular advancement splint as an alternative to CPAP if they: are aged 18 and over and have optimal dental and periodontal health.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.5 Treatments for mild OSAHS
Mandibular advancement splints for mild OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that semi-customised mandibular advancement splints may be inappropriate for people with: active periodontal disease or untreated dental decay few or no teeth generalised tonic-clonic seizures. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on treatments for mild OSAHS . Full details of the e

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: See also section 4 on providing information for people starting treatment for OSAHS .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
CPAP for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: CPAP is recommended as a treatment option for adults with moderate or severe symptomatic OSAHS in NICE's technology appraisal guidance on continuous positive airway pressure for the treatment of obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome (TA139, updated 2021) .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
CPAP for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer fixed-level CPAP, in addition to lifestyle advice, to people with moderate or severe OSAHS.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
CPAP for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with moderate or severe OSAHS having CPAP: Offer telemonitoring with CPAP for up to 12 months. Consider using telemonitoring beyond 12 months.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
CPAP for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider auto‑CPAP as an alternative to fixed-level CPAP in people with moderate or severe OSAHS if: high pressure is needed only for certain times during sleep or they are unable to tolerate fixed-level CPAP or telemonitoring cannot be used for technological reasons or auto‑CPAP is available at the same or lower cost than fixed-level CPAP, and this price is guaranteed for an extended period of ti

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
CPAP for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider heated humidification for people with moderate or severe OSAHS having CPAP who have upper airway side effects such as nasal and mouth dryness, and CPAP-induced rhinitis.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
CPAP for moderate and severe OSAHS
Reducing the risk of transmission of infection when using CPAP
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that CPAP is an aerosol-generating procedure and, if there is a risk of airborne infection, such as COVID‑19, appropriate infection control precautions should be taken. These may include setting up the device at home by video consultation or with precautions in hospital. For more information, see the UK government guidance on COVID-19: infection prevention and control and local guidance.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
Mandibular advancement splints for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: If a person with moderate or severe OSAHS is unable to tolerate or declines to try CPAP, consider a customised or semi-customised mandibular advancement splint as an alternative to CPAP if they: are aged 18 and over and have optimal dental and periodontal health.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.6 Treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS
Mandibular advancement splints for moderate and severe OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that semi-customised mandibular advancement splints may be inappropriate for people with: active periodontal disease or untreated dental decay few or no teeth generalised tonic–clonic seizures. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on treatments for moderate and severe OSAHS . Full d

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.7 Further treatment options for OSAHS
Positional modifiers for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider a positional modifier for people with mild or moderate positional OSAHS if other treatments are unsuitable or not tolerated.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.7 Further treatment options for OSAHS
Positional modifiers for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that positional modifiers are unlikely to be effective in severe OSAHS. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on positional modifiers for OSAHS . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review H: positional modifiers .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.7 Further treatment options for OSAHS
Surgery for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider tonsillectomy for people with OSAHS who have large obstructive tonsils and a body mass index (BMI) of less than 35 kg/m 2 .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.7 Further treatment options for OSAHS
Surgery for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referral for assessment for oropharyngeal surgery in people with severe OSAHS who have been unable to tolerate CPAP and a customised mandibular advancement splint despite medically supervised attempts. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on surgery for OSAHS . Full details of the e

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.8 Managing rhinitis in people with OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Assess people with nasal congestion and OSAHS for underlying allergic or vasomotor rhinitis.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.8 Managing rhinitis in people with OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: If rhinitis is diagnosed in people with OSAHS, offer initial treatment with: topical nasal corticosteroids or antihistamines for allergic rhinitis or topical nasal corticosteroids for vasomotor rhinitis.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.8 Managing rhinitis in people with OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with OSAHS and persistent rhinitis, consider referral to an ear, nose and throat specialist if: symptoms do not improve with initial treatment or anatomical obstruction is suspected.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.8 Managing rhinitis in people with OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that: rhinitis can affect people's tolerance to CPAP but changing from a nasal to an orofacial mask and adding humidification can help (see recommendation 1.5.5 on heated humidification for mild OSAHS and recommendation 1.6.4 on heated humidification for moderate and severe OSAHS ) CPAP can worsen or cause rhinitis and nasal congestion. For a short explanation of why the committee made th

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Tailor follow-up to the person's overall treatment plan, which may include lifestyle changes and treating comorbidities. See the recommendations on tailoring healthcare services for each patient in the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for people using CPAP
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer face-to-face, video or phone consultations, including review of telemonitoring data (if available), to people with OSAHS having CPAP. This should include: an initial consultation within 1 month and subsequent follow-up according to the person's needs and until optimal control of symptoms and apnoea–hypopnoea index (AHI) or oxygen desaturation index (ODI) is achieved.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for people using CPAP
paragraph_or_text_span: Once CPAP is optimised, consider annual follow-up for people with OSAHS.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for people using CPAP
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with OSAHS having CPAP access to a sleep service for advice, support and equipment between follow-up appointments.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for people using mandibular advancement splints
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer face-to-face, video or phone consultations, including review of downloads from the device (if available), to people with OSAHS using a mandibular advancement splint. This should include: initial follow-up to review adjustment of the device and symptom improvement at 3 months and subsequent follow-up according to the person's needs and until optimal control of symptoms and AHI or ODI is achie

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for people using positional modifiers
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer face-to-face, video or phone consultations, including review of downloads from the device (if available), to people with OSAHS using a positional modifier. This should include: an initial consultation within 3 months and subsequent follow-up according to the person's needs until optimal control of symptoms and AHI or ODI is achieved.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for people who have had surgery
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with OSAHS who have had surgery: an initial follow-up consultation with respiratory polygraphy within 3 months of the operation and subsequent follow-up according to the person's needs.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Follow-up for drivers with excessive sleepiness
paragraph_or_text_span: Ensure follow-up is in line with Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency guidance on assessing fitness to drive . For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on follow-up for people with OSAHS . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review L: monitoring .

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Monitoring treatment efficacy
paragraph_or_text_span: Assess the effectiveness of treatment with CPAP, mandibular advancement splints and positional modifiers in people with OSAHS by reviewing the following: OSAHS symptoms, including the Epworth Sleepiness Scale and vigilance, for example, when driving severity of OSAHS, using AHI or ODI adherence to therapy telemonitoring data or download information from the device (if available).

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Monitoring treatment efficacy
paragraph_or_text_span: Explore with people using CPAP their understanding and experience of treatment, and review the following: mask type and fit, including checking for leaks nasal or mouth dryness, and the need for humidification other factors affecting sleep disturbance such as insomnia, restless legs and shift work sleep hygiene cleaning and maintenance of equipment.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.9 Follow-up and monitoring for people with OSAHS
Monitoring treatment efficacy
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider stopping treatment if OSAHS may have resolved, for example, with significant weight loss. After at least 2 weeks without treatment: re-evaluate any return of symptoms and consider a sleep study. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on monitoring treatment efficacy in people with OSA

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.10 Supporting adherence to treatment for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with OSAHS educational or supportive interventions, or a combination of these, tailored to the person's needs and preferences, to improve adherence to CPAP, mandibular advancement splints and positional modifiers.

heading_path: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
1 Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome
1.10 Supporting adherence to treatment for OSAHS
paragraph_or_text_span: Interventions to support adherence to treatment for OSAHS should be given by trained specialist staff when treatment is started and as needed at follow-up. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on supporting adherence to treatment for OSAHS . Full details of the evidence and the committee's d
